parameter: Exterior Design; Infotainment Display; Interior Design; Hybrid Option; Interior Design; Transmission; Head Up Display; Parking Assist; Accessories
content_english:
Lucky it hasn't changed much from the old car... turns out the climate control cluster and touch multimedia are actually great. They're much nicer to use than those physical buttons. After using it for a while, I find it very convenient and really good... only regret is the old model was supposed to have a hybrid but Vietnam didn't offer it, while the new one does... the leather interior looks worse than the brown of the old model... and the 8-speed diesel engine... kkk, the Smartstream 8-speed diesel drives really well... smooth... the 8-speed gearbox feels amazing, the car shifting through gears when you turn on Sport mode is super satisfying. Also, the HUD—too bad the Seltos has it but this one doesn't. The Korean version has it, even from the old model... plus the ability to manage it via phone like the Carnival... that got cut too...

The rear cargo hook—looks flimsy but it's actually really sturdy. I bought this car partly because of that, hanging bags or a tent is very convenient, I've used it since 2022 and it hasn't broken yet. In short, this car is only about a 20% upgrade over the old model, not much.....

content_english:
LUCKY it hasn't changed too much compared to the old car... overall, the climate control and touchscreen multimedia are good. I prefer that over physical buttons. Over time, I find it very convenient and nice... just a shame the old version was supposed to have a hybrid but Vietnam didn't make it, while the new version does have it... the leather interior looks worse than the brown of the old model... and the 8-speed DCT... haha the SmartDream 8-speed DCT drives very well... smooth... the 8-speed shifts feel amazing, the car shifts up when you engage Sport mode, very satisfying. Plus, it's a pity the Seltos has HUD but this one doesn't. The Korean version does... has it since the old model... and the blind-spot view monitor like the Carnival also got cut...

The rear load hook looks flimsy but it's actually sturdy... I bought this car partly because of that, to hang a motorcycle helmet or tent, very convenient, used from 2022 to now, hasn't broken. In short, this model is only about a 20% upgrade over the old version, not much.....

content_english: Driving this car gives a vibrating sensation when pressing the accelerator, and the gear shifts feel jerky because it's essentially a pure gasoline vehicle—it runs more on fuel than electricity and still uses a traditional transmission. The only advantage Korean cars have over Chinese cars is their global brand recognition and longer history.

parameter: Front End Design; Turn Signal Design; Rear End Design; Wheel Design; Wheel Design; Interior Design; Parking Sensors; Dashboard Layout; Infotainment Display; Connectivity; Suspension; Headlight Design; Price
content_english: As a user of the 2022 Tucson (pre-facelift), when I look at the facelift version, I find the front end to be more refined than the old model. However, the turn signals on the facelift have two large bulbs on each side, so they'll definitely be more noticeable on the road. The rear bumper of the pre-facelift also looks better, and as for the wheels, I think the 2022 model has nicer designs (except for the base trim where the new wheels look better). On the top 1.6T variant, the 2025 no longer comes with the black headliner and brown seats like the 2022. Otherwise, the 2025 is a big improvement: it adds more sensors around the car, a more modern dashboard, a larger and sharper screen, all ports switched to USB-C, a new suspension system that absorbs bumps better, the 1.6T has brighter LED headlights, and importantly, the listed price for the 2025 is considerably lower than the 2022.

parameter: Air Conditioning; Ventilated Seats; Remote Start
content_english: I'm currently driving a 1.6T 2022 model. On days when it's 37°C or hotter, when I get to the car while it's been sitting in the sun, I have to run the fan at nearly max speed with the temperature at 16-20°C for about 5-10 minutes before it cools down. After that, I still have to keep the fan on 3-4 and the temperature at 22°C. It's not as deeply cool as Japanese car A/C, but it's not so weak that it's unusable. Oh, and the two front seats have ventilation, so sitting is much more comfortable. And when it's hot, you should remote start the car in advance to let it cool down and warm up the engine.

content_english: If you're torn between the Tucson 2025 and the Sportage, let me share a comparison based on what I've seen and noted (my younger sibling also drives a Sportage 1.6T): On this new generation, the suspension on the Tucson is similar to the Sportage (the previous Tucson 2022 had a softer suspension than the Sportage). The engine and transmission are shared, so the driving feel is identical. Many say the sound insulation and speakers on the Sportage are better than the Tucson, but I didn't notice a difference between the two. The interior tech is exactly the same, though the Sportage offers a brown leather color (which I prefer more). It has handy little extras like coat hooks for the rear seats (Sportage), and the Sportage's rear charging port is positioned quite conveniently. All Sportage trims come with the same 19-inch rims, pre-installed running boards, and the A-pillar is also more spacious. The Sportage's bi-LED headlights are brighter, and its rear turn signals are mounted higher, making them easier for cars behind to see. The Sportage's hood has gas struts, while the Tucson requires a manual prop rod. Where the Sportage falls short is that only the front two windows have one-touch auto up/down (the rear two do not), while the Tucson has one-touch on all four windows. Also, the Sportage doesn't have a quick-adjust button for the front passenger seat from the rear, and it seems to lack cornering lights (the Tucson has them only on the 1.6T trim). Apple CarPlay/Android Auto on the Sportage requires a wired connection via USB-A, or you can buy a wireless adapter kit, but that introduces a slight delay. On the 1.6T trims, both cars use a dry dual-clutch transmission, so they are prone to overheating when shifting frequently (common in traffic with constant shifts from N/P to 1st and 2nd gear; when overheated, the car will display a warning and refuse to move until the transmission cools down). The 1.6T engine accelerates very quickly/strongly, though there's some initial turbo lag. On the 1.6T trims, both cars have all-wheel drive, so cornering feels quite "sharp," and driving in the rain gives more confidence since the system controls all four wheels to reduce slipping on slippery roads (but this AWD system isn't meant for off-roading).

content_english: Air conditioning is not the problem. The problem is that in 2026-2027 there will be a completely new Tucson model. It's up to you whether you want to buy or not, so think about it. It's a really good deal. I own a special version right now—there's nothing to complain about. It's spacious and comfortable, the ride is smooth, sound insulation is good, plenty of features. That's all you need. If you get the turbo or diesel version, it's even better. As for the air conditioning, to be honest, in the summer I always set it to 22-24 degrees with fan speed 1 or 2 (except on days when the car has been sitting in the sun—then I have to set it to 17-18 degrees with full fan, and after a few minutes it goes back to 22-24). Even the air conditioner in the bedroom takes a few minutes to cool down, so why bother about the car's AC? Why pay attention to such small details? The CX-5 is about to launch a new model—bigger screen, longer body, probably more spacious too. It's worth waiting for that one.

content_english: Nice car, but I advise anyone in the city planning to buy a car to stay away from the 1.6 Turbo version with the dry 7-speed dual-clutch transmission that is "prone to failure". This dual-clutch transmission tends to overheat when stuck in traffic. In mild cases the car starts shaking and jerking; in severe cases it warns that the gearbox is too hot, forces you to pull over, and won't let you drive anymore. I don't understand why Kia (and Hyundai too) would produce and sell such a faulty powertrain. The other day I was stuck in Hanoi traffic for just 40 minutes and the car immediately gave a warning and made me stop. Spending all that money to buy a car that can't even move in traffic is absolutely foolish!

category: Styling / Design; Brand Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Exterior Design; Brand Reliability; Brand Image
content_english:
1. Xiaomi is not Korean.
2. Samsung now is also starting to imitate Apple by reducing design changes to optimize production lines. For example, look at the S21 and S22, they only updated the camera bump, and I think the S23 will also keep the same frame and same lineup to optimize production lines, just like Samsung is doing with the Z Fold and Z Flip series.
3. Before, people often said Kia and Hyundai had no design language, no connection between design generations. But in recent years, they recruited some designers from Germany and announced a proper design language (as some foreign media mentioned). Look at BMW: no matter the generation, it still looks like a BMW. So don't worry about design; the important thing is whether you like it or not.
4. Now the concern about Hyundai and Kia should be whether the engines are durable or not, whether there are minor defects or not... I remember reading somewhere that in recent years, Hyundai and Kia changed their engines, so we should worry and observe how it goes.
5. Durability depends on the user, car beauty depends on the viewer. If I personally like it, no matter what others say, I like it and that's enough. Personally, I think Japanese cars are the ones with outdated designs (except Mazda). But Japanese cars are durable, so buying a car also depends on your needs.
6. => Don't be quick to use a brand's past to judge their efforts to change. Come back in 5, 6, or 7 years to objectively evaluate whether the 2022 Korean cars were done well or not.

content_english: I went to see it in person and found that the KIA Sportage diesel engine feels superior and more appealing than the Hyundai Tucson, from exterior design to interior and tech features. First, the Sportage's design has been greatly improved, with a modern, sleek look thanks to the German engineers' design; even the Kia logo looks nicer and more abstract than before. The lights are seamlessly integrated into the body, fully LED, with electric tailgate and hydraulic struts to prevent slamming—very convenient. Inside, the legroom is much more spacious, the seatbacks recline comfortably for all 5 passengers, and the perforated leather seats are breathable and cool. In short, the design looks somewhat reminiscent of luxury cars, very pleasing to the eye. As for the engine, the KIA Sportage diesel has a turbo from 2.0L up to 2.8L, handy for climbing hills or rough, bumpy roads needing strong torque. Additionally, the engine has a remote start/stop feature via key fob, convenient...

content_english: I dropped the CX5 and chose between the Tucson and the Sportage. The Sportage has a bold, sporty, cool design but lacks elegance. The Tucson has a bold yet refined and more luxurious design. I compared them very carefully—the Sportage is extremely plasticky, the lower part has very large plastic trims, the interior has a lot of plastic, especially the AC surround around the hazard light button is extremely cheap three-cent plastic, and the sunroof rubber trim is just okay. The Sportage built by Thaco has a higher localization rate than the Tucson. The Tucson has a more refined, luxurious interior with a premium feel and much better fit and finish than the Sportage. Most guys only like the Sportage interior because of the curved screen—nothing impressive otherwise. Uncle Thang, the Kia fan, needless to say. If you want luxury and premium, skip the Sportage. I've already settled on the Tucson 1.6 Black and will receive it in November.

content_english: Sound insulation? Honestly, the Tucson isn't as good as the Sportage. The reason is the hard tires — you can really hear the tire noise. To fix it, swap out the four tires. However, compared to other rivals like the CR-V and CX-5, the Tucson's sound insulation is noticeably better. At this price point, it's hard to ask for more. I've also experienced the full diesel Santa Fe and found the insulation is about the same — sometimes the Santa Fe is even louder in the engine, and the diesel engine's whine is much more annoying than the Tucson's. In terms of practical value for the money, the Tucson does better.

The main reason is that the initial selling price of the Sportage is significantly higher than the Tucson, so buyers thought it was overpriced, and even when it later dropped in price, they no longer cared. Another factor is its somewhat radical design, which looks a bit odd and appeals to a narrower market than the Tucson.

However, for that price difference, the same trim (e.g., 2.0D) of the Sportage comes with noticeably more equipment: 19-inch rims, side steps, roof rails, tires, seats, a fully integrated screen, and a clearly thicker body. You can feel the difference just by closing the doors of the two cars.

I test-drove both cars thoroughly, and in the end, I decided to go with a Wave Alpha.

content_english:
The main reason is that the initial selling price of the Sportage is quite a bit higher than the Tucson, so people think it's overpriced, and even when they later drop the price, they no longer care. The next factor is the exterior design, which is somewhat unconventional and a bit goofy, making it more niche than the Tucson.

However, in return for that price difference, the same trim level (e.g., 2.0D) of the Sportage comes with noticeably more features: 19-inch wheels, fender flares, roof rails, tires, seats, an integrated screen, and visibly thicker body panels. You can tell just by closing the doors on both cars.

I thoroughly tested both of these cars, and in the end, I decided to go with a Wave Alpha.

parameter: Infotainment Display; Sound System; 360 Camera; Interior Design; Interior Features; Hood Struts; Driver Visibility; Front Visibility; Lighting System; Engine and Chassis; Wheel Size; NVH; ADAS Features; Cruise Control; Option Value; Exterior Design - Rear; Exterior Design - Front; Exterior Design - Front (Facelift)
content_english: The main reason is that the Sportage is better. It's better mainly in terms of features: the Sportage has a larger infotainment screen, I like listening to lively music so I prefer Harman over Bose, which needs a bit of tuning. The 360 camera is sharper. The Sportage interior is more thoughtfully designed, with many details that serve the user well. The AC control screen or other button displays, Type C charging ports. The hood has gas struts. The A-pillar angle is more open because the mirrors are mounted on the doors. It's also easier to see the front of the car when driving. The Sportage lighting system is better. The engine and chassis are identical, I'm just an average person with little driving experience, so I can't tell the difference. All Sportage trims come with 19-inch wheels. Both cars have nearly the best noise insulation in the segment. ADAS, lane centering on both cars is extremely good. Both have ACC and LIM. So the Tucson isn't lacking in anything, but if you want better features, you pay more for the Sportage. As for exterior, I like the Tucson rear and Sportage front. I don't like the Sportage rear or Tucson front. The Tucson Facelift coming to Vietnam soon has a much better front end.

content_english: The main reason is that the Sportage is more expensive. It's pricier mainly due to the extra features — the Sportage has a larger infotainment screen, and since I like listening to upbeat music, I prefer Harman over Bose (Bose needs a bit of tweaking). The 360-degree camera is sharper. The Sportage interior is more thoughtfully designed, with many user-friendly details. The climate control screen or button display is different, and it has USB-C charging. The hood comes with gas struts. The A-pillar blind spot is smaller because the mirrors are mounted on the doors. Sitting in the driver's seat and seeing the front of the hood is also easier. The Sportage's lighting system is better. The engine and chassis are identical — I'm a regular person with limited car experience, and I've test-driven too few times to tell the difference. All Sportage trims come with 19-inch wheels. Both cars have nearly the best sound insulation in their segment. ADAS and lane centering are excellent on both. Both have ACC and LIM. So the Tucson is not lacking anything either; you just pay more for the extra features on the Sportage. As for exterior, I like the Tucson's rear and the Sportage front. I don't like the Sportage's rear and the Tucson's front. The upcoming Tucson Facelift coming to Vietnam has a much better front end.

content_english: Went to the showroom to check out 3 cars. The Sportage had a terrible smell, so immediately eliminated it without a second thought. Test drove the CX-5 Premium and the Tucson 1.6; clearly the Tucson is much stronger but feels jerky, probably due to the turbo. The CX-5 drives in a gentle, smooth, and refined manner. For the interior, the Tucson has more plastic than the CX-5, the screen has quite a few touch buttons which look cluttered, but it's better with AWD, a full panoramic sunroof, and the second row can recline. The CX-5's interior looks simple, more leather gives it a sophisticated feel, but the screen is only 8 inches—usable but still feels small. Oh, and the HUD looks extremely premium. The leather quality on both cars is about the same. If two months ago the CX-5 didn't have ventilated/heated seats and a complete ADAS package, I would have bought the Tucson without hesitation. So in the end, I chose the CX-5 simply because maintenance is cheaper and I'm not quite confident in Hyundai's gasoline engine. If the diesel had AWD, I would have chosen it right away.
